content.text: Connectors are the mechanism by which Cowork tasks access external data sources. Without connectors, a task can only read from the project workspace and from the goal text itself. With connectors, it can query Google Drive, read Slack channels, fetch Jira issues, browse GitHub repositories, and access other connected systems — all at task execution time, without you having to paste data into the goal manually.
How Connectors Work

attrs.alt: Cowork connector architecture: six external services connect to the Cowork engine via OAuth-scoped access grants; connector data enters the task context during step execution

content.text: A connector is an OAuth-authorized integration between a Cowork project and an external service. When you set up a connector, you go through an OAuth flow that grants the Cowork engine a specific, limited set of permissions on your behalf — for example, read access to one Google Drive folder or read access to specific Slack channels. The engine never gets your password or full account access; it only gets the scope you explicitly authorized.

At task execution time, when the engine reaches a step that references a connector, it makes a read request to that service using the stored OAuth token. The response — document text, message content, issue data, file contents — is inserted into the task's context window as text. From that point, the engine reasons over it like any other content.
Available Connector Types
Cowork supports several categories of connectors, each providing read access to a different class of external data.
Connector
Data Provided
Typical Use in Tasks
Google Drive
Document and file contents from authorized folders
Read specs, reports, templates; fetch meeting notes
Slack
Messages from authorized channels
Surface recent discussions, decisions, and action items
Jira
Issues, sprints, and projects from authorized projects
Fetch ticket status, open bugs, sprint progress
GitHub
Repositories, pull requests, issues, and file contents
Read code context, PR descriptions, issue lists
Google Calendar
Events from authorized calendars
Read upcoming meetings, deadlines, scheduling context
Chrome Extension
Current browser tab content and clipboard
Feed page content directly into a task without copy-paste
Confluence
Pages and spaces from authorized spaces
Read internal documentation and wiki content
Salesforce
CRM records, accounts, and opportunities
Fetch account status, deal stage, contact history
What Connectors Are Not
Understanding what connectors do not do is as important as understanding what they do. Common misconceptions:

Connectors are read-only. The Cowork engine reads data through connectors; it does not write back to the connected service unless a specific write capability is explicitly part of the connector (Slack posting is an exception for some configurations).

Connectors do not give the engine full account access. The OAuth scope you grant during setup is the ceiling. If you granted read access to one Drive folder, the engine cannot read other folders even if they exist.
Connectors do not run continuously. They fetch data on demand when a task step requests it — there is no background sync or real-time stream.
Connectors are not a database. They return text snapshots of data at query time. If the underlying source changes mid-task, the engine uses the snapshot it already fetched.

Connectors do not replace workspace uploads. For data that needs to persist between runs (CSVs, templates, state files), use the project workspace — connectors are for fetching from live external systems.
Connector Scope and Permissions
The scope of a connector determines exactly what the engine can access. Scope is set when you authorize the connector via OAuth and cannot be expanded without re-authorizing.
Principle of Least Privilege

Grant the narrowest scope that meets your task requirements. A connector granted access to your entire Google Drive can expose sensitive documents to any task in the project. A connector scoped to one folder limits exposure to that folder's contents.
Connector
Narrow Scope (recommended)
Wide Scope (use with caution)
Google Drive
Specific folder (e.g. /Reports/Q2/)
Entire Drive including all personal files
Slack
Specific public channels (e.g. #analytics)
All channels including private ones
Jira
Specific project (e.g. project ANALYTICS)
All projects in the Jira workspace
GitHub
Specific repository (e.g. org/analytics)
All repositories in the organization
Google Calendar
One calendar (e.g. work calendar)
All calendars including personal ones
Scope at Task Execution Time

The engine uses whatever scope was granted when the connector was authorized. Narrowing the scope in a task goal (by saying 'only read the /Q2/ folder') is a best-effort instruction — the engine will follow it, but it cannot access anything outside the OAuth grant. If you want the engine to access a new folder you added to Drive, you need to re-authorize the connector with updated scope.

Think of the OAuth grant as a key that unlocks a specific cabinet. Telling the engine 'only look at shelf 3' is a useful narrowing instruction, but the key still only opens that cabinet — not the whole building.
Setting Up a Connector
Connectors are configured at the project level. Every task in the project can use any connector that has been added to the project's connector list.
Open the Cowork project settings.
Navigate to the Connectors tab.
Select the connector type you want to add (Google Drive, Slack, Jira, etc.).
Click 'Authorize' to begin the OAuth flow for that service.
In the OAuth dialog, sign into the service and grant the requested permissions. Review the scope carefully — this determines what the engine can access.
After authorization, the connector appears as active in the project settings.
Test the connector by creating a task goal that explicitly references it (e.g. 'Fetch the latest file from Google Drive /Reports/' and confirming the engine retrieves it.
Referencing Connectors in Task Goals

To use a connector in a task, reference it explicitly in the goal text. The engine does not automatically use all available connectors — it only fetches from a connector when the goal specifically instructs it to.
Goal Pattern for Connector Use

# Explicit connector reference:
Fetch the most recent report from Google Drive folder /Reports/Q2/
(connector: Google Drive).

Read Slack messages from #analytics-team posted in the last 7 days
(connector: Slack).

# The engine will only use these specific connectors for these specific queries.
# Other authorized connectors in the project are not touched by this task.
Scoping Connector Queries in the Goal

Even though the OAuth scope is the hard limit on what the connector can access, scoping queries in the goal prevents the engine from retrieving more data than the task needs, which manages context window budget.

# Narrow query (recommended):
Fetch Jira issues in project ANALYTICS where status = Open and priority = High
(connector: Jira). List only: issue key, title, assignee, days since created.

# Wide query (use only when all data is needed):
Fetch all Jira issues in project ANALYTICS across all statuses
(connector: Jira).
Connector Data in Context
Connector-retrieved data is inserted into the task's context window as text at the point in the task where the connector call happens. This has practical implications for task design.
Consideration
Detail
Design Implication
Context window budget
Large connector results (many files, long threads) consume significant token budget
Scope queries narrowly; ask for summaries rather than full content when possible
Data freshness
Data is fetched at the moment the step executes; it is a snapshot, not a live stream
For time-sensitive data, run the task close to when the data was last updated
Multiple connectors per task
A task can use multiple connectors; each result is added to context as fetched
Each connector call adds to budget; plan for total context use across all calls
Order of retrieval
Connector data is added to context in the order the steps request it
Put the most important connector call first so it is in context before other large content
Connectors vs Workspace Files

Workspace files and connectors serve different roles. Choosing the right one for each data type prevents both over-reliance on connectors (which adds latency and context cost per run) and under-use (which requires manual file management).
Data Type
Use Connector
Use Workspace File
Live data that changes each run (metrics, tickets, messages)
Yes — connector fetches fresh each time
No — would require manual re-upload each run
Reference data that is stable (templates, dictionaries, config)
No — wasteful to fetch unchanged data each run
Yes — upload once; engine reads from workspace
Large datasets (thousands of rows)
No — too large for context window
Yes — pre-filter to workspace before task
Prior run outputs (for comparison)
No — connector does not provide prior task outputs
Yes — workspace accumulates run history
External auth-required data (CRM, project tools)
Yes — connector handles auth
No — cannot directly upload from authenticated systems
Connector Availability Across Platforms
The connector ecosystem in Cowork is evolving. The available connectors and the specific OAuth scopes supported may vary by Cowork plan and by platform (web vs desktop app).
Core connectors (Google Drive, Slack, Jira, GitHub) are available across standard Cowork plans.
The Chrome extension connector is available when using the Cowork desktop app or browser extension.
Enterprise connectors (Salesforce, Confluence, custom integrations) may require specific plan tiers.
New connectors are added as integrations are built; check the Cowork project settings for the current list of available connector types in your account.
If a connector you need is not available, contact Anthropic or use the workspace file approach: export data from the source manually, upload to /workspace/, and reference the file in the goal.
Connector-Specific Usage Patterns

Each connector type has characteristic query patterns that match how the underlying service structures its data. Writing connector calls that match these patterns produces cleaner results than generic requests.
Google Drive Connector Patterns

Drive organizes data in folders. The most reliable query strategy is to reference a specific folder path and let the engine enumerate the files it finds there. Vague queries ('read my Drive') produce unpredictable results.

# Reliable Drive query patterns:

# By folder:
Read all files in Google Drive /Strategy/Q2-Planning/ (connector: Google Drive).

# By file name:
Read the file named 'Q2 Revenue Analysis.docx' from Google Drive
(connector: Google Drive, folder: /Reports/).

# With recency filter:
List all files in Google Drive /Reports/ modified in the last 30 days
(connector: Google Drive). For each, read the first 500 words.

# Avoid:
Read everything relevant to Q2 revenue from my Google Drive.
# (Too vague — engine will pull unpredictably)
Slack Connector Patterns

Slack data is organized by channel and time. Always specify both — channel name and date range — to prevent over-retrieval. Slack threads can be verbose; ask for messages that match specific keywords when the channel is high-traffic.

# Reliable Slack query patterns:

# By channel and date:
Fetch messages from #analytics-team posted in the last 14 days
(connector: Slack).

# With keyword filter:
Fetch messages from #product that contain 'pricing' OR 'subscription'
posted in the last 30 days (connector: Slack).

# Thread context:
Fetch the top-level messages from #engineering posted this week.
For any message with more than 3 replies, include the full thread.
(connector: Slack)

# Avoid:
Read #general for the last 6 months.
# (High-traffic channel over long period = massive context cost)
Jira Connector Patterns

Jira data is structured as issues with attributes (status, priority, assignee, labels). The most effective queries combine project scope with at least one attribute filter. Jira can return thousands of issues if unfiltered — always include a status or priority filter.

# Reliable Jira query patterns:

# By project and status:
Fetch all open Jira issues in project ANALYTICS
(connector: Jira, filter: status = Open).

# By assignee:
Fetch Jira issues in project ANALYTICS assigned to @sarah.chen
where status != Done (connector: Jira).

# By label or sprint:
Fetch Jira issues in project ANALYTICS labeled 'customer-reported'
updated in the last 14 days (connector: Jira).

# By priority:
Fetch all Critical and High priority open issues across project ANALYTICS
(connector: Jira). For each, extract: key, title, assignee, days open.
GitHub Connector Patterns

GitHub data includes pull requests, issues, and file contents. When referencing file contents, specify the file path and branch. For PRs and issues, use state (open/closed/merged) and date filters to avoid pulling the entire repo history.

# Reliable GitHub query patterns:

# Pull requests:
Fetch open pull requests in repository org/analytics
(connector: GitHub). For each, extract: PR number, title, author, age, file list.

# File content:
Read the file src/reports/config.yaml from the main branch
of repository org/analytics (connector: GitHub).

# Issues with label:
Fetch open GitHub issues labeled 'data-quality' in repository org/analytics
created in the last 30 days (connector: GitHub).

# Recent merges:
Fetch pull requests merged into main in the last 7 days
in repository org/analytics (connector: GitHub).
Connector Error Handling in Goals

Connector calls can fail for several reasons: token expiration, rate limits, changed OAuth scope, or service downtime. A task goal that does not account for failure will either silently skip the connector data or produce a misleading output. Write explicit failure handling for every connector call.

# Connector failure handling pattern:
Fetch open Jira issues in project ANALYTICS (connector: Jira).

If the connector call fails or returns an error:
  Write to the output: 'Jira connector unavailable — issue data not included in this report.'
  Continue with remaining sections using other sources.
  Do not fabricate issue data.

# Specific handling for empty results:
If the connector returns 0 issues:
  Write: 'No open issues found in project ANALYTICS as of this run.'
  Do not interpret this as an error — it is valid data.
Connector Error Type
Likely Cause
Goal Instruction Pattern
Authorization error
OAuth token expired or revoked
Note 'connector auth error' in output; do not guess at data
Zero results returned
No data matches the query filter; valid state
Write 'No results found' explicitly in the relevant section
Partial results (truncated)
Result set too large; service returned first N items
Note 'results may be truncated' in output; scope query more narrowly next run
Timeout
Service slow or overloaded
Note 'connector timeout — section skipped'; continue other sections
Permission denied for specific resource
Scope grants access to folder X but not folder Y
Note which resource was inaccessible; do not infer its contents
Managing Multiple Connectors in One Project
A project can have multiple connectors authorized simultaneously. All tasks in the project can access any authorized connector. This shared model has implications for project design.
Use a naming convention in goals to make connector use explicit: always write '(connector: Slack)' or '(connector: Jira)' after a retrieval instruction so it is clear which system is being queried.
Audit connector scope quarterly: OAuth grants persist until revoked. Remove connectors that are no longer used by any task in the project to reduce the project's attack surface.
Separate sensitive connectors into separate projects: if one project handles HR data and another handles engineering metrics, keeping their connector grants separate prevents accidental cross-access.
Document which connectors each scheduled task uses: add a comment at the top of the goal listing the connectors required, so future reviewers can verify the OAuth grants are still valid.
Test connector availability before enabling a scheduled task: run the task manually once and verify each connector returns expected data before setting it to run automatically.
Connector Security Model

Connectors use OAuth 2.0, the industry standard for delegated authorization. The engine never receives or stores your credentials — only the OAuth access token that the service issues after you authorize.
Security Property
What It Means in Practice
Delegated authorization
You authorize the token; Anthropic stores it encrypted; you can revoke it at any time from the connected service's OAuth settings
Scope-limited access
The token only grants the permissions you approved; the engine cannot escalate beyond that scope
No credential storage
Your password never passes through Cowork; OAuth tokens are all that is stored
Per-project grants
A connector authorized in project A cannot be used in project B; grants are project-scoped
Revocation
Revoke access anytime by removing the connector in Cowork settings or revoking the OAuth grant in the service's own account settings

Revoking a connector token in the connected service (Google, Slack, Atlassian, etc.) immediately prevents the engine from using that connector, even if it is still listed in the Cowork project settings. Use this as an emergency brake if you suspect a token was used inappropriately.
Summary
Connectors are OAuth-authorized integrations that let Cowork tasks read from external services (Google Drive, Slack, Jira, GitHub, and others) at task execution time.
Access is bounded by the OAuth scope granted during setup — the engine cannot access data outside the authorized scope.
Connectors are read-only, on-demand, and snapshot-based — they are not background syncs or write channels.
Grant the narrowest scope that meets your task requirements to limit exposure of sensitive data.
Reference connectors explicitly in task goals; the engine does not use connectors unless the goal instructs it to.
Scope connector queries in the goal to manage context window budget even when the OAuth grant allows broader access.
Use connectors for live external data; use workspace files for stable reference data and accumulated history.
